161.showDesc: Aysanabee is a multi-instrumentalist, producer and singer-songwriter currently based in Toronto. He is Oji-Cree, Sucker Clan of the Sandy Lake First Nation, a remote fly-in community in the far reaches of Northwestern Ontario. In a short amount of time, three singles were released including “Ego Death” and “Nomads”, which charted on Billboard Canada and became #1 on CBC Music and went #1 on Alternative Radio, making Aysanabee the first Indigenous artist to reach #1 on any Mediabase chart. His album Watin was shortlisted for the Polaris Music Prize in 2023. Aysanabee’s latest EP, Here and Now, moves in a new direction, towards his own experiences of love’s end and his process of unflinching self-examination.

162.showDesc: Toronto-based vocal powerhouse Serena Ryder is a platinum-selling artist adored by fans, peers and critics alike, in part due to her raw and earnest songwriting, and beautifully electric live performances. She has won seven prestigious JUNO Awards, including the 2022 Adult Contemporary Album of the Year for her record, The Art of Falling Apart, which invites listeners to join her mental wellness journey and helps us understand the importance of sitting with the uncomfortable moments and the wisdom in their messages.

163.showDesc: Now boasting over two billion global streams across platforms, Rainbow Kitten Surprise first began building their devoted fanbase with independent albums. As if channeling another dimension where genres simply don’t exist, the group finds harmony in unpredictability – weaving together lyrical poetry, hummable melodies, and a rush of instrumental eccentricities. The North Carolina-bred quartet’s forthcoming album, LOVE HATE MUSIC BOX, ponders life’s ups and downs, and traces the turbulent trajectory of relationships, painted out loud in hues of pop, electronic, rock, and hip-hop.

164.showDesc: Hailing from the hollers of southern Indiana, Stephen Wilson Jr. has taken a winding path to his musical career, making stops as a Golden Gloves boxing finalist, scientist, and indie rock band member before finding his voice as a solo artist. His genre-defying debut double album, søn of dad, cemented him as a force in country and Americana music. The 22-song collection, dedicated to his late father, was named the Best Album of 2023 by Holler and All Country News, and hailed by Rolling Stone as “the most existential release of the year.”

165.showDesc: Grammy and JUNO award-winning artist Allison Russell is a weaver of stories and tales through the medium of music. Since the release of her first solo album three years ago, the self-taught singer-songwriter, poet, activist, and multi-instrumentalist has redefined what artistry means in the 21st century. From her devastatingly moving celebration of survivors' joy through, Outside Child, to the body-shaking, mind-expanding, soulful expression of Black liberation that is, The Returner, Russell’s music exceeds all reasonable (and unreasonable) expectations and affirms her place among today’s most vital artists.

417.showDesc: Calvin Vollrath was raised on Don Messer’s Jubilee and at the age of eight, received his first fiddle. At the age of 13, he entered his first fiddle contest and by 17, he was already winning the Championship class. Vollrath has been twice crowned the Grand North American Old Tyme Fiddle Champion. To date, he has composed over 800 tunes, many of which have become standard contest and dance songs across North America & Europe. He has to his credit, 76 of his own albums, numerous music books of his original compositions, an instructional DVD and was commissioned to compose five fiddle tunes for the Vancouver Winter Olympics 2010 Opening Ceremonies.

425.showDesc: For over a decade, Current Swell have earned a global following thanks to their electrifying performances and soulful songwriting. The Victoria, BC six-piece band – led by Scott Stanton and David Lang, alongside Louis Sadava, Marcus Manhas, Phil Hamelin, and Dave St. Jean – have toured the globe, captivating audiences with their infectious melodies, and a blend of indie rock, folk, and a touch of West Coast roots. After an extended break since 2019’s album, Buffalo, Current Swell returns with their highly anticipated EP, Peacekeeper; a release that reminds the world why they’ve become such a beloved force.

455.showDesc:
Martyn Joseph is a completely unique and mind-blowing artist.

Take everything you think you know about singer-songwriters and rip it up. For one man and a guitar he creates a performance with a huge far-reaching sound that is energetic, compelling, and passionate. Be it to 200 people or 20,000, he blows the crowd away night after night. 2024 kicked off with the release of a new “masterful and career defining” (Fatea UK) studio album, This Is What I Want To Say, along with a sell out UK tour, followed by shows in mainland Europe and North America.

461.showDesc:
School of Song (Canada/AB)

Asiah Holm (Canada/AB) Asiah Holm is a Cree First Nations singer-songwriter who fuses her Indigenous roots with indie, folk, pop, and ambient electronic influences to create rich emotive soundscapes anchored by powerful melodies, soul-stirring vocals, and intimate storytelling. In just two years, Asiah has released two EPs, multiple singles, and a full-length album. Her EP, Self-Portrait, earned her two Native American Music Award nominations, a Top 10 spot on the Indigenous Music Countdown, a nomination for “Single of the Year” at the 2025 Canadian Folk Music Awards, and a feature on Apple Music’s Indigenous Now Playlist. A true architect of her own sound, Asiah writes, records, produces, and mixes all of her own music from her home studio.

Halfway Home (Canada/AB) Halfway Home is Amanda Penner and Zackery Wilson Elliott, who both have spent the last few years living in various people’s spaces, connected over the readiness for something new in their lives and careers. They decided to join forces in what has become Halfway Home, an Edmonton based folk duo influenced by the likes of Adrianne Lenker, Gregory Alan Isakov, and Watchhouse. Their songs explore the concept of home and what it means to them as new roommates and duo partners. With years of solo careers under their belts, a blend of spellbinding harmonies, and blissful acoustic guitar sounds, Halfway Home is bound to leave listeners tapping their toes and humming along.

Sam Steffen (Canada/AB/USA) Sam Steffen has described his songs as torch-wood for a new generation of folk music that has learned from the best the tradition has to offer, and aims to keep the human spirit alive and kicking. Originally from eastern Pennsylvania, Steffen’s versatile musicianship, finger-picking, and storytelling have made him an outstanding voice in the Americana folk genre. His songs have been featured in radio programs, podcasts, academic lectures, political protests, worship services, bars, restaurants, coffeehouses, concert venues, festivals, street corners, weddings, funerals, and living rooms. He recently moved to Canada from the US and currently lives in Edmonton, Alberta with his wife and dog.

Tayler Grace (Canada/AB) Tayler Grace is an Edmonton-based folk singer-songwriter whose soulful, heartfelt music resonates with listeners on a deeply emotional level. Drawing inspiration from artists like Gregory Alan Isakov, The Paper Kites, and other storytellers of the indie-folk genre, Grace’s sound is a rich tapestry of haunting melodies and poetic lyrics, inviting listeners to reflect, heal, and feel. Her ability to connect with listeners on a personal level has made her a rising musician in the local indie folk scene. Following the 2024 release of her debut EP, Let You Go, Grace is set to take her music to even greater heights, sharing her passion and vulnerability with fans old and new.
tags: School of Song (Asiah Holm, Halfway Home, Sam Steffen, Tayler Grace)

462.showDesc: Secondhand Dreamcar is a 9-piece soul and roots band fronted by Canadian Folk Music Award-nominee Dana Wylie and also featuring notable producer Harry Gregg on bass, Jamie Cooper (Up and Over Trio, Obsessions Octet) on drums, Kyle Mosiuk (The Hello Darlins) and Chad Murphy (Dallas Smith) on guitar, Rooster Davis on organ, plus a horn section made up of some of Canada's best – Dave Babcock on sax, Bob Tildesley on trumpet, and Peter Filice on trombone. This group of Alberta heavies is crafting a distinct, funky, and lyrically driven sound that journalist and programmer Peter North has deemed the most exciting thing to come out of Edmonton since k.d. lang and The Reclines.

464.showDesc: Steph Strings is a multi-instrumentalist who tells a story through her music. With influences from the John Butler Trio, Tash Sultana and Ziggy Alberts, this multi-instrumentalist entails bursts of percussion alongside Celtic, blues and coastal fingerstyle indie-rock. 2020 saw Steph perform an incredible sold out show at Melbourne’s iconic Chapel off Chapel venue, as well perform shows with The Pierce Brothers, Kim Churchill, Ash Grunwald and many more. Steph is a uniquely gifted, emerging talent that is a must see.

468.showDesc: Wesli, a Haitian-born and raised artist now thriving in Canada, stands out as a versatile singer, multi-instrumentalist, and producer who has become one of Haiti's most inspiring musical ambassadors. His unique compositions seamlessly blend traditional Haitian rhythms with an eclectic array of styles that reflect his broad musical influences and experiences. The JUNO and Canadian Folk Music Awards winning artist’s ability to fuse the vibrant sounds of his homeland with contemporary genres have captivated audiences worldwide.

166.showDesc: Las Cafeteras have taken the music scene by storm with their infectious live performances and have crossed many genres and borders along the way. Their electric sound and energy has taken them around the world playing shows at Bonnaroo, the Hollywood Bowl, WOMAD New Zealand, the Montreal Jazz Festival, and many more. Born and raised East of the Los Angeles River, Las Cafeteras are remixing roots music as modern day troubadours. They are a sonic explosion of Afro-Mexican rhythms, electronic beats and powerful rhymes that document stories of a community seeking to ‘build a world where many worlds fit.’

167.showDesc: Taj Mahal has pursued and elevated the roots of beloved sounds with boundless devotion and skill. He's not only a god to rock-and-roll icons such as Eric Clapton and The Rolling Stones, but also a hero to ambitious artists toiling in obscurity who are determined to combine sounds that have heretofore been ostracized from one another. A 2025 Grammy win for, Swingin' Live at the Church in Tulsa, brought his Grammy tally to five wins and 17 nominations, and underscored his undiminished relevance more than 50 years after his solo debut. Blues Hall of Fame membership, a Lifetime Achievement Award from the Americana Music Association, and other honours punctuate his résumé.

168.showDesc: Raised on the music of Tammy Wynette and Dolly Parton, Goldie Boutilier’s music is noted for its cinematic quality and exploration of tragic romance, glamour, and melancholia. Her upcoming EP, The Actress, promises to be her best release yet, with a writing process that sent her on a journey of integrity, purity, vulnerability, and a healing return to herself. These are Boutilier’s true tales of the power struggles to be in control of her own career, being exploited by “The Man”, discovering how much she could sacrifice for her dreams, and finding the strength to keep pushing forward, despite it all.

169.showDesc: At 39, Danielle Ponder gave up a successful career as a public defender to focus purely on her music. By 2022, she signed her first record deal, and became a trailblazer on her own unusual path, developing a distinct style centered around her commanding voice. A once-in-a-generation talent, Ponder’s soulful and genre-defying sound and unique backstory are self-evident. Infused with a myriad of influences, from the gospel she grew up singing, to trip-hop, blues, pop, R&B, and alt-rock, Ponder brings them together organically, effortlessly and powerfully.

170.showDesc: Formed in 1987 in Philadelphia, PA, The Roots have become one of the best known and most respected hip-hop acts in the business. Named one of the "50 Greatest Live Acts" by Rolling Stone, The Roots became the official house band on "The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon" where they currently perform every Monday-Friday. "They are not merely the world’s best hip-hop band—but one of the great musical outfits of our time." (Rolling Stone)

411.showDesc: Blue Moon Marquee writes and performs original compositions that carve a path through blues, jazz, jump jive, folk, country, swing and Indigenous soul. A.W. Cardinal (vocals, guitar), Jasmine Colette (vocals, bass, drums) and Darcy Phillips (piano, organ) are gifted at bringing all these elements together without anything sounding out of step. The Maple Award winning group collects roots and smoothly braids them with lyrics that often touch on the underbelly of society, woven with elements of Indigenous storytelling and poetic cadence.

420.showDesc: Certainly, plenty of things have happened in Cedric Burnside’s life since he went on the road at age 13, drumming for his grandfather, the pioneering bluesman R.L. Burnside. His two albums before, I Be Trying – 2015’s Descendants of Hill Country and 2018’s, Benton County Relic – were both nominated for Grammys. He has become a recipient of a National Heritage Fellowship, the country’s highest honour in the folk and traditional arts and was recently recognized with the 2024 Mississippi Governor's Art Award for Excellence in Music. His latest album, Hill Country Love, was nominated for a 2025 Grammy for "Best Traditional Blues Album".

428.showDesc: Using Québec’s vibrant living music tradition as a springboard for musical innovation, De Temps Antan is a power trio that catapults audiences headlong into the future of French-Canadian music and culture. Éric Beaudry, David Boulanger and Pierre-Luc Dupuis have been exploring and performing time-honoured melodies from the stomping grounds of Québec’s musical past. Using fiddle, accordion, harmonica, guitar, bouzouki and a number of other instruments, these three virtuosos blend boundless energy with the unmistakable joie de vivre found in traditional Québec music.

431.showDesc: Steeped in musical traditions, all six members of Dervish marry technical brilliance with a rare sensibility, which could only have been born from an understanding of the music and many years of playing together. Dervish are long-established as one of the biggest names in Irish music internationally. They’re renowned for live performances, which match dazzling sets of tunes with stunning interpretations of traditional songs. Their studio and live albums – 13 to date – make up one of the outstanding catalogues in Irish music. “[Their] integrity makes the group a formidable heir to the throne abandoned by The Bothy Band, if not a serious contender for The Chieftains’ international space.” (The Irish Times)

433.showDesc: At the heart of Dry Bones are three accomplished musicians: J.D. Edwards, Nathan Rogers, and Leonard Podolak, each a force in their own right. Together they are a powerhouse trio that seamlessly blends contemporary songwriting with deep traditional roots. Their music is both fresh and timeless—new wine in an old bottle, where echoes of the past weave through every note. One moment, they channel the raw energy of a jug band; the next, they ignite with the driving force of acoustic rock and roll. Their performances are dynamic, their songwriting exceptional, and their instrumental interplay nothing short of brilliant.

438.showDesc: Where Heynderickx’s debut album, I Need to Start a Garden was about self-actualization and the excavation of the soul, her new album, Seed of a Seed is now about protecting it. Her answer is by going inwards, requiring a brave solitude, while also acknowledging that it’s not done alone. This album nods to the necessary helpers along the way—from flowers (“Gemini”) and daydreams (“Foxglove”) to forests (“Redwoods (Anxious God)”) and friends (“Jerry's Song”). Seed of a Seed is an album that honours these helpers, the self, and the process of fighting upward toward the light.

448.showDesc: Formed at school in Dublin’s late 1980’s, award-winning band, Kíla, are heralded as one of Ireland’s most innovative and exciting bands. Their unique sound has been ever-evolving, rooted in tradition, yet inspired by a myriad of influences and ideas from all around the world. Often defined as contemporary Irish World music, Kíla fuse the effects of their own melodies and Irish lyrics with Irish folk instruments. The group’s eight members come from the differing musical backgrounds of traditional, classical, and rock, resulting in a fresh blend of freewheeling instrumentals, furious jigs, and primal rhythms that transcend the traditional boundaries of Irish music.

450.showDesc: His decade-long industry experience spreads far and wide. Raised in the coastal town of Merimbula, Australia, Kim Churchill has previously been crowned Australia’s National Youth Folk Artist of the Year and nominated for the National Live Music Awards Roots Act of the Year. 2024 saw Churchill touring Europe and his largest headline tour of Canada to date with stand out performances at EFMF and Mariposa Folk Festival. His latest single ‘Reflecting’ featuring Canadian born, LA based folk singer Emily Brimlow is part of a new album, It’s Lovely To Have You Here, which was released in October, 2024.

451.showDesc: Māmā Mihirangi & the Māreikura is a powerful and fierce all-female Māori world-roots production, a captivating journey that celebrates the rising divinity of feminine power and cultural identity. Fusing ancestral wisdom, healing, and empowerment through contemporary performance, led by internationally renowned artist/activist and the pioneering Queen-of-Loops, Māmā Mihirangi. Regarding their live performance, “I was personally overcome by an incomprehensible, powerful force… their mesmerizing fractal flow and otherworldly theatrics invoked a rapturous level of being that only the purest music is able to conjure." (Roots Music Canada)

452.showDesc: Madi Diaz has been making records and writing songs professionally since the late 2000s, but it wasn't until she released 2021's History Of A Feeling that she felt the glare of wider notoriety. She made her daytime and nighttime television debuts, embarked on her first solo tour since 2014, and toured and collaborated with Waxahatchee and Angel Olsen. On her latest album, Weird Faith, Diaz once again examines a romantic partnership, but this time, her songs are about falling for someone and the endless self-questioning a new relationship inspires. "After being really burned by love — maybe relentlessly burned by it — the album is about being brave and trying again. Doing it differently," she says. "It's in our nature to try to be brave like that.”

453.showDesc: Mama's Broke is a powerful duo that deliver a compelling performance with heart and raw energy. Although highly influenced by their Canadian roots, Lisa and Amy are based out of nowhere and everywhere. Their two strong voices blend to create haunting harmonies, while they artfully juggle fiddle, banjo, guitar and mandolin, and incorporate traditional dance and foot percussion into their performances. Their original, and often dark compositions push the boundaries of tradition and the constraints of genre. Drawing from old-time, Quebecois, blues, punk, celtic, balkan and doom metal, they create a soundscape that is both familiar and new.

454.showDesc: A young blood with an old soul, Marcus Trummer marries modern sensibilities with tradition in his distinctive style that encompasses blues, soul, and rock. Dubbed “a precocious guitarist whose vocals possess a wise-beyond-his-years weariness” (Calgary Herald), the Calgary-born artist is already earning recognition. In 2022, Trummer snagged a nomination for "New Artist of the Year" at the Maple Blues Awards. The following year he traveled to Colorado to perform as a finalist in the 2023 Telluride Blues Challenge, which he would go on to win.

457.showDesc: As she has so eloquently accomplished over the past 25 years, acclaimed singer-songwriter Mary Gauthier has used her art once again to traverse the uncharted waters of the past few years. “I’m the kind of songwriter who writes what I see in the world right now,” she affirms. Thankfully, amid dark storms of pandemic loss, she found and followed the beacon of new love: her gift to us, the powerful, Dark Enough to See the Stars, collects ten sparkling jewels of Gauthier’s songcraft reflecting both love and loss. According to Gauthier, “Writing helps me sort out confusion, untangle powerful emotions, and ward off desperation. It helps me navigate the powerful emotional weather systems of life.”

460.showDesc: Each accomplished solo artists, Bill MacKay and Riley Walker have come together to make excellent instrumental duo recordings of interlocking guitars and warm give-and-take. “On [the song] “I Heard Them Singing,” from SpiderBeetleBee, the duo’s second album of guitar duets, their partnership results in contagious joy, audible in every note. When left alone, Walker crafts cosmic folk music that reverberates with a pensive haze; here, his playing is weightless and breezy. Atop Walker’s fingerpicking, MacKay plays a five-string requinto guitar with a nimble, romantic voicing.” (Pitchfork)

463.showDesc: Shaela Miller’s years spent doing good country music work are safely etched into public consciousness.  A turn at this point is by no means an undoing, but a welcome lead into new territory.  Miller’s fast-rising path is set to be amplified by a sonic reintroduction to one of Alberta’s best voices and most sincere stage personalities. With her latest release, After The Masquerade, Miller explores a dual world of New Wave and Classic Country — a self-indulgent trip back to a world of music she’s been patiently waiting to explore.

466.showDesc: The Sensational Barnes Brothers are a melting pot of the old and the new. “The brothers run through a spectrum of moods and modes in their gospel soul, often bridging the gap between, say, the Soul Stirrers and Stax, all the while keeping a local flavour.” (PopMatters) The brothers are able to dive deep into their roots, creating a sound that reflects the music of their history, all the while drawing in a modern-day audience.  From playing in their family living room to recording their first album from a legendary 1970s catalog, The Sensational Barnes Brothers have created a place for themselves in music.

467.showDesc: The JUNO nominated and Canadian Folk Music Award winning group are Canada’s bluegrass band to watch. Rooted in tradition, fearlessly creative and possessing a bold, dynamic sound, The Slocan Ramblers have become a leading light of today’s acoustic music scene. With a reputation for energetic live shows, impeccable musicianship and an uncanny ability to convert anyone within earshot into a lifelong fan, the group has been winning over audiences from Merlefest to RockyGrass and everywhere in between.

647.showDesc: Nîpisîy is an Indigenous women’s ensemble blending Indigenous tradition and contemporary vocal harmonies with the rhythms of the rattle and the drum in the spirit of happiness, health and healing. Nîpisîy, meaning willow in Cree, perfectly embodies the authentic intention of combining four veteran vocalists to create a powerful and melodic collaboration which intertwines the strength of women’s voices. Together these Northern songbirds, Debbie Houle, Donita Large and Cindy Paul are sure to sing their way into your heart.

706.showDesc:
Stage 7 comes alive on Saturday morning with a special performance by Nîpisîy ᓂᐱᓯᔨ.

Join us again this year as the Edmonton Folk Music Festival continues to work with members of Edmonton’s Indigenous Arts Community to present First Nations, Métis and Inuit artists on our stages. We acknowledge that the EFMF takes place on Treaty 6 traditional lands, the home of the Cree, Dene, Blackfoot, Saulteaux, Nakota Sioux and Metis peoples. We look forward to growing our relationship with Indigenous peoples and communities in the coming years and we invite you to join us as we walk this journey of reconciliation together.

171.showDesc: The Rebel General and his pyrotechnicians have drawn from all the rhythms of Congo since their debut to reveal their superpowers–rock and funk, ready for any union and every invitation. They have even conquered Latin America and joyfully reinvented the triangular trade of sounds. It's obvious for someone who, from his very first record, proclaimed, "Ich bin ein Congolese, The World is my land". "We all have ancestors in Africa", Jupiter reminds us, "who also practiced music. And if Africa resembles a revolver, its trigger is found... in Congo". From Kinshasa, Jupiter and his merry men give the signal–a new journey around the world awaits.

172.showName: Richy Mitch & The Coal Miners Concert
172.showDesc: Balancing folk songwriting, rock 'n' roll energy, raw instrumentation, and sonic wanderlust, the Seattle based musicians draw inspiration from the friendship between them. What began as a high school passion project in Colorado Springs has quietly translated to billions of streams and packed crowds. In 2023, the one-minute and 27-second intro to RMCM, "Evergreen," caught fire on TikTok, and has since generated over half a billion Spotify streams. They've graced the bills of festivals such as Lollapalooza, Under The Big Sky, and Hinterland, have collaborated with Mt. Joy and Caamp, and remained prolific with new singles "Signal Sender," "Careful," "Northstar," and "Sierra Vista.".

richy-mitch-the-coal-miners-concert.name: Richy Mitch & The Coal Miners Concert

173.showDesc: John Butler is no pie-in-the-sky hippy. He has dirt beneath his feet: red dirt. He is well-known Down Under for his environmental and political commitment - putting his money where his mouth is. Founder of Jarrah Records, a family man and proud skateboard aficionado, Butler remains an enigma. He is from everywhere and nowhere, an Australian/American, whose music mixes rootedness and rootlessness, pain and celebration in a way that is utterly beguiling. He is the consummate rebel-refugee whose songs chart disenchantment with the corporate world and show a yearning for truth along with an ongoing struggle for a sense of locus.

174.showDesc: Forming in 2016, the band made their mark two years later when their self-titled, Mt. Joy, debut album spun off the platinum single "Silver Lining," an uplifting power ballad that went to #1 on Adult Album Alternative (AAA) radio. Subsequent releases, Rearrange Us and Orange Blood, have drawn widespread critical raves. “Mt. Joy's songs unfold like good political speeches: They amble and converse and pulsate fervently until it's time to get the crowd chanting along.” (NPR)

408.showDesc: BALTHVS is Balthazar Aguirre (guitar, vocals), Johanna Mercuriana (bass, vocals) and Santiago Lizcano (drums, vocals). Formed in 2020, the group has grown in just five years to become Colombia’s leading alternative international act. With over 45 million streams on digital platforms, and nine international tours under their belt, the band has performed at all major Colombian festivals (Festival Centro, Rock al Parque, Estéreo Picnic) and international festivals like Austin City Limits, Outside Lands, Electric Forest, and SXSW Sydney, also making notable appearances on prestigious radio platforms, including KEXP, Gilles Peterson’s Worldwide Café, and the BBC.

410.showDesc: Blackburn Brothers have graced stages alongside an illustrious lineup of globally renowned icons, from the legendary Mavis Staples and Dr. John and The Nite Trippers to the electrifying talents of Christone "Kingfish" Ingram and Cedric Burnside. Their latest album, SoulFunkn'BLUES, has received international acclaim, winning the 2024 Blues Blast Award for Soul Blues Album and earning a nomination for Blues Band of the Year. It also garnered a 2024 JUNO Award nomination for Blues Album of the Year and set a new precedent for Canadian bands with three 2024 American Blues Music Awards nominations: Album of the Year, Soul Blues Album of the Year, and Song of the Year. Additionally, the album achieved a record-setting nine nominations at the 2024 Maple Blues Awards, including Entertainer of the Year and Electric Act of the Year.

416.showDesc: The Manitoba based Indigenous duo features Nadia and Jason Burnstick, esteemed independent artists who united over a decade ago to create deeply moving, cinematic folk music using their trademark vocal pairing, and Jason's mastery of the lap slide Weissenborn guitar. Five years after their nationally acclaimed debut album, Kîyânaw, Burnstick unveils Made of Sin, their remarkable and poignant sophomore album. With thought-provoking lyrics and engaging stories, they often leave listeners reflecting on their own experiences, feelings, and memories.

424.showDesc: Charlie Cunningham has emerged as a truly under-the-radar success story. Splitting his time between piano and nylon string guitar, Cunningham’s melancholic yet rhythmically driven songs have seen him become a regular fixture in Europe's most prestigious concert halls. His new album, In Light, offers a luminous blend of gently pulsating organic production, soaring melodies, and Cunningham’s signature masterful guitar work. The arrangements, understated yet dynamic, respond instinctively to his voice, weaving in unexpected key changes, moments of distortion, and atmospheric flourishes.

436.showDesc: Elizabeth Moen’s voice is a powerhouse, but it’s in the quiet moments—when she whispers—that her true strength shines. With a style that blends folk music with an indie-rock edge, her music is raw and real, drawing from her own experiences to craft lyrics that are both deeply personal and incredibly relatable. Since her debut album in 2016, Moen’s music has resonated with listeners, earning her a place in the hearts of fans and critical praise from outlets like American Songwriter and No Depression. With the making of her 2022 album, Wherever You Aren’t, she found a calling and an opportunity to reflect life’s lessons into a gripping statement of art.

440.showDesc: Ian Noe draws on the day-to-day life of Eastern Kentucky on his debut album, Between the Country, where 10 original songs introduce a number of complicated characters, diverse in their own downfalls but bound together by Ian’s singular voice. "I've always thought that Eastern Kentucky had a certain kind of sound, and I can't really explain it any better than that," he says. "What I was trying to do was write songs that sounded like where I was living."  With his second album, River Fools & Mountain Saints, “Noe still wears his principal [John Prine] influence on his sleeve, but “Ballad of a Retired Man” could as easily come from Nebraska-era Springsteen, and “PoW Blues” from John Fogerty.” A gritty, compelling talent.” (The Guardian)

441.showDesc: Jeffrey Martin’s fourth full length album, Thank God We Left The Garden, was produced and engineered by the man himself, recalling, "There was a magic quality to the sounds I was getting in the shack with these two cheap microphones, some lucky recipe of time and place that allowed my voice and the way I play guitar and the shape of these new songs to come together with the kind of honesty I was craving." The Portland-based singer-songwriter writes “songs that are stark in their simplicity, yet emotionally rich in a way that can catch your breath in your throat or leave your eyes suddenly damp.”

443.showDesc: Camelot, Castle’s 2024 album is a chronicle of the artist in early middle age, charting a more real, more rooted, and more metaphorical place than the fabled Camelot of the Early Middle Ages. Her seventh record is at once her most monumental and unguarded to date, demonstrating a mastery of rendering her verse and melodies alike with crisply poignant economy. “Castle’s singing carries the joy of life. It’s what helps keep the artist perched on the vanguard of celestial country and fantastical folk, a title she affirms once again.” (The Fader)

444.showDesc: Based in Victoria, BC, Jesse Roper is an artist with a penchant for writing modern indie infused blues music and delivering bombastic live performances buoyed by his virtuosic guitar playing. His innate and impressive musical talent has served him well in recent years. The stage is where Roper shines, and has been his home since overcoming crippling stage fright during his early twenties. Roper’s new album, Horizons marks a sonic high point in his career thus far.

445.showDesc: Already an underground rock ‘n’ roll influence, Jesse Welles disappeared and re-emerged a folk wordsmith; a quick witted bard on acoustic guitar, his voice reflecting a hard-sung existence, though it’s hard to tell with Welles, his disarming smirk can lead you to doubt even the most black and white evidences. His lyrical range (from a doomed Whitman to an optimistic McCarthy, from pacifistic aggressions to oppressively bleak honesties) keeps up with his fingers; his guitar work would be enough in itself for most artists.

447.showDesc: Julianno Riolino knows how to capture and highlight beauty before it fades. On her debut LP, All Blue, Riolino reflects on her own past, the memories of pain, healing, and love strewn through it. The songs focus morality and the stretch of time, seeping naturally into Riolino’s Americana-indebted songwriting, resulting in a golden and fluid debut. Blending past and present musically represents Riolino’s own experience, as well, the songs written over a period of years, their meanings picking beyond that stretch and pulling lessons forward. And in that process, her philosophical lyrics bring that complexity forward to the listener in surreally sweet melodies, pouring growth and healing directly through the ear and into the heart.

449.showDesc: Ken Pomeroy creates a wild but safe space of her own – one that is brutally honest, proudly Native American, and undeniably brilliant. Her outstretched hand to the wounded manifests as startlingly good songs. Pomeroy’s soprano is comforting, almost sweet, but perhaps most powerful delivering a devastating line. A deft guitarist, she opts for beds of rootsy strings that can soothe or haunt. Her song, “Wall of Death” made its way onto the Twisters soundtrack, while Hulu’s Reservation Dogs featured her soul-mining gem, “Cicadas”.

458.showDesc: With her latest album, Borned In Ya, Melissa Carper showcases her long-standing influences as well as her artistic growth and sense of adventure. In addition to her familiar blend of country with jazz, blues, soul, and R&B, the new album sees Carper exploring a more subtle and expert crossing of these genres and with a matured lyrical depth. According to Chris Scruggs, “she’s as good as it gets. She has a quality that really transcends time and fashion.”

459.showDesc: Sage Motel, Monophonics’ fifth studio album since 2012, tells the story of the once thriving, once vibrant establishment of the same name. A place where folks experience the highs and lows of human existence. Produced by brilliant bandleader Kelly Finnigan, the album captures a timeless sound that blends heavy soul with psych-rock. With their previous album, It’s Only Us, garnering over 20 million streams, Monophonics have built a reputation over the past decade as one of the most impactful bands in the genre.

465.showDesc: One of Scotland’s most popular folk-based groups to emerge in the last decade, chart-toppers Talisk have toured the world stacking up major awards for their explosively energetic yet artfully woven sound, including Folk Band of the Year at the BBC Alba Scots Trad Music Awards, a BBC Radio 2 Folk Award, and the Belhaven Bursary for Innovation. Mohsen Amini, Hayley Keenan and Graeme Armstrong fuse concertina, fiddle and guitar to produce a ground-breaking, multi-layered and instantly enthralling signature sound that has captivated audiences around the globe.

469.showDesc: For folk singer Willi Carlisle, singing is healing. And by singing together, he believes we can begin to reckon with the inevitability of human suffering and grow in love. His latest album, Critterland, is a wild romp through the backwaters of his mind and America, lingering in the odd corners of human nature to visit obscure oddballs, dark secrets, and complicated truths about the beauty and pain of life and love. “If we allow ourselves to sing together, there's a release of sadness, maybe even a communal one. And so for me personally, singing, like the literal act of thinking through suffering, is really freeing,” he says.

470.showDesc: When guitarist and composer Yasmin Williams sits down to compose music, she doesn’t scour her subconscious for unheard melodies or clever chord progressions. Instead, she goes granular—fixating on a single note. She’ll play it over and over, sustaining it, varying the attack or the release to change its essence, eventually adding notes to form chords. “Nobody plays the guitar like Yasmin Williams. [Her latest album, Acadia] takes her sound into new territory … Worth every minute of an almost four-year wait.” (The Fader)

644.showDesc: With an unmistakable voice like “gravel on silk”, and a colourful landscape of tightly-crafted lyrics, driving rhythms and uplifting melodies, JUNO nominated singer-songwriter  AHI (pronounced “eye”) is creating folk music the whole world can sing to. From charting on Spotify, Apple Music, and Amazon, to delivering captivating performances on CBS & NPR Tiny Desk, AHI has earned over 100 Million streams worldwide, propelling him to the forefront of today’s folk and roots music scene.
